title: Raddoppiamento sintattico fonologico
pronunciation_features_introduced: raddoppiamento sintattico fonologico (RSF) as cross-word gemination
RSF Class 1: stressed monosyllables (a, e, è, ho, ha, tre, che, chi, più, già, su, fra, tra, me, te, sé)
RSF Class 2: polysyllables with orthographic final accent (città, caffè, perché, così, però, parlò, sarà)
contrast with non-triggering unstressed clitics (il, la, di, in, con, per, non)
gemination is invisible in spelling — an orthographic absence with phonological presence
perceptual parallel to French liaison: same binding instinct, different mechanism (segmental resurrection vs. length)
regional distribution: consistent in Tuscany / Rome / south, weaker / absent in the north
cognate_patterns_showcased: French liaison vs. Italian RSF as two varieties of cross-word sandhi
stressed-monosyllable triggering in Italian vs. register-graded liaison in French
notes: Fourth A1 pronunciation lesson. Builds on doppie consonanti from 002-consonanti by displacing the gemination contrast across a word boundary. French liaison is used as the framing analogy since the target audience is B2-Francophone; the parallel is drawn explicitly with a comparison table, and the mechanism difference (liaison surfaces a latent segment; RSF lengthens an initial consonant) is called out. No drill-eligibility compromises: every Italian example is a ≥3-char phrase, so all examples drill cleanly. The regional caveat (north weakens or drops RSF) is stated explicitly to pre-empt learners who hear non-gemination in Milanese/Torinese speech.
